phrases: Good morning, it's nice to see you
Hi there, how's your day going?
Good afternoon, how have you been?
Good evening, how's your day been?
Hi, it's good to catch up with you
Hello, how have you been lately?
Hi there, hope you're having a good day
Hi, how are you holding up?
Hey there, how's your week been?
Hi, it's been a while, how are you?
Good afternoon, how's life treating you?
Hey, nice to see you again, how have you been?
It was lovely to meet you
We should do this again sometime
It was a pleasure, hope to see you again
Say hi to everyone for me
Take care and stay in touch
It was so good catching up with you
Nice to meet you, I'm glad we connected
Is there anything I can do for you?
traductions: Buenos días, es un placer verte
Buenos días, espero que estés bien
Buenas noches, ¿cómo ha estado tu día?
Hola, es bueno ponerse al día contigo
Hola, espero que estés teniendo un buen día
Hola, ha pasado un tiempo, ¿cómo estás?
Buenas tardes, ¿cómo te trata la vida?
Oye, es bueno verte de nuevo, ¿cómo has estado?
Fue un placer, espero verte de nuevo
Qué bueno fue ponernos al día
Mucho gusto, me alegra que nos hayamos conocido
¿Hay algo que pueda hacer por tí?
grammar: Contraction tip: 'it's' = 'it is'. Never use 'its' (possessive) for this.
'How's' = 'How is'. Present continuous ('going') describes an ongoing state.
'How have you been?' uses the present perfect to ask about a period up to now — not just this moment.
Subject drop in casual speech: 'Morning, hope you're well' omits 'Good' and 'I' — common in informal greetings.
Idiomatic use: 'treat' here means 'to deal with / affect'. 'How is life treating you?' = 'Is life going well for you?'
'Catch up' (phrasal verb) = to exchange news after time apart. Common collocations: 'catch up with someone', 'catch up on news'.
'Holding up' (phrasal verb) = coping, managing under pressure. Implies checking in with empathy.
'It's been a while' uses present perfect to connect the past to the present. Contrast: 'It was a while ago' (simple past — finished).
Exclamatory sentence: 'It was great seeing you!' uses the gerund after 'seeing'. Compare: 'It was great to see you!' — both correct, gerund is slightly more casual.
'Take care of yourself' uses a reflexive pronoun. 'Take care' alone is also common as a farewell.
'See you around' implies you'll meet again without fixing a time. More casual than 'See you later' or 'See you soon'.
Note the difference: 'Nice to meet you' (first time) vs 'Nice to see you' (you've met before). Mixing them is a common mistake.
'Don't be a stranger' is an idiomatic farewell meaning 'keep in touch / visit again'. Not literal.
'We should do this again' — modal 'should' here is a suggestion, not an obligation. Very common in social farewells.
'Say hi to everyone for me' — indirect greeting via a third person. 'Say hi', 'give my regards', 'send my love' vary in formality.
'Stay in touch' (phrasal verb) = keep communicating. Synonyms: 'keep in touch', 'keep me posted'.
'Catch up' revisited: 'catching up' as a gerund after 'good'. Compare phrase 8: 'catch up with you'.
'Could be better' is a polite understatement meaning 'I'm not great'. English often uses understatement to avoid being negative.
Present perfect 'I've had' links a past event to the present feeling. Contrast: 'I had a great day' (simple past, already over).
'I've had better days' is an idiomatic understatement = 'today was bad'. Very common polite complaint.
'So' as an intensifier: 'Why are you SO happy?' adds emphasis. It can also signal mild surprise.
'What's on your mind?' is an idiom meaning 'What are you thinking about / worried about?'
'Is there anything I can do for you?' — formal offer of help. Informal version: 'Can I help you with anything?'
